refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts command latency, feedback fidelity, and operator situational awareness—all safety-critical factors in remote heavy equipment control. Refinement clarifies that the requirement is conditional on 'agreed network conditions' and ties acceptance to a measurable, customer-negotiated target rather than a fixed value.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under representative network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth constraints) agreed with customer. Simulate degraded network scenarios (WiFi, 4G/5G, satellite uplinks as applicable). Measure end-to-end packet loss across the teleoperation data path (operator input → vehicle command → vehicle telemetry → operator display). Acceptance: measured packet loss ≤ customer-defined limit for ≥95% of test duration.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall detect and report packet loss events in real-time.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall implement error correction or retransmission mechanisms to minimize effective packet loss impact on command execution.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall gracefully degrade or alert the operator when packet loss exceeds acceptable thresholds.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The network link shall be provisioned and monitored to maintain packet loss within customer-defined limits under agreed conditions.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement adaptive forward error correction (FEC) and selective retransmission in the teleoperation protocol stack, with real-time packet loss monitoring and operator alerting.
DP-B: Deploy redundant communication paths (dual network links) with automatic failover to reduce single-point-of-failure packet loss impact.
DP-C: Implement application-level packet prioritization and quality-of-service (QoS) negotiation with the network provider, combined with local buffering and jitter compensation.
DP-D: Combine DP-A (FEC/retransmission) with DP-C (QoS/buffering) to address both protocol-level and application-level packet loss mitigation.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a multi-layered packet loss mitigation strategy combining (1) adaptive forward error correction and selective retransmission at the teleoperation protocol layer, (2) application-level QoS negotiation and prioritization, (3) local buffering and jitter compensation, and (4) real-time packet loss monitoring with operator alerting and graceful degradation triggers.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation protocol design (FEC/retransmission logic) with network link provisioning (QoS negotiation). This is acceptable because both are necessary to meet the packet loss requirement; however, the coupling must be managed via well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, Network Link) to avoid tight architectural binding.
DP-D also couples operator alerting (Safety Services domain) with teleoperation monitoring. This is appropriate given the high safety criticality of packet loss in remote equipment control.
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP avoids over-specification of a single mechanism (e.g., FEC alone) and instead provides a balanced, defense-in-depth approach. Coupling is justified by the safety-critical nature of the requirement and the need for redundant mitigation strategies.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer will define and agree on 'network conditions' (e.g., latency range, bandwidth, jitter profile) before detailed design. Risk: Vague network conditions lead to ambiguous acceptance criteria.

Assumption: The teleoperation protocol stack is modular enough to integrate FEC and retransmission without major redesign. Risk: Legacy protocol implementations may not support adaptive error correction.
Assumption: Network provider can support QoS negotiation and monitoring. Risk: Some networks (e.g., public cellular) may not offer QoS guarantees.
Assumption: Operator alerting and graceful degradation do not introduce unacceptable latency. Risk: Mitigation logic itself could increase latency and worsen packet loss perception.
Risk: Redundant communication paths (failover) increase system complexity and cost; cost-benefit must be validated against customer requirements.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly separate concerns: (1) Protocol-level mitigation (FEC/retransmission) owned by Teleoperation module, (2) Network-level QoS provisioning owned by Network Link module, (3) Operator alerting owned by Safety Services module. This separation reduces coupling and clarifies ownership boundaries.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the teleoperation protocol stack, including adaptive forward error correction, selective retransmission, application-level QoS negotiation, local buffering, jitter compensation, and real-time packet loss monitoring. The module interfaces with the Vehicle module (via Tasking for command transmission and Telemetry for feedback reception) and with Safety Services (via SafetyAlert for operator alerting on packet loss degradation).

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives vehicle telemetry (sensor data, state feedback) from Vehicle module. Teleoperation module monitors packet loss on this inbound stream and applies jitter compensation and buffering.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Sends operator commands (movement, tool control, mode changes) to Vehicle module. Teleoperation module applies FEC and retransmission logic to this outbound stream.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Sends packet loss alerts and graceful degradation notifications to Safety Services module when packet loss exceeds thresholds. Enables operator alerting and potential operational restrictions.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Interfaces with network provisioning and monitoring services. Teleoperation module negotiates QoS parameters and receives network condition feedback (latency, jitter, available bandwidth) to adapt mitigation strategies.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of protocol-level packet loss mitigation (FEC, retransmission, buffering, jitter compensation). The module is cohesive around teleoperation communication quality. Minor diffusion: Network Link interface requires coordination for QoS negotiation, but this is a well-defined external dependency, not diffusion.

Vehicle module owns the physical/logical vehicle and its control systems. Packet loss mitigation is not a vehicle-specific concern; it is a communication infrastructure concern. Assigning DP-D to Vehicle would scatter protocol logic across vehicle firmware, creating high diffusion and poor cohesion.

Safety Services owns safety policies, alerting, and operational constraints. While operator alerting on packet loss is a safety concern, the core mitigation logic (FEC, retransmission, QoS) is not a safety service responsibility. Assigning DP-D to Safety Services would conflate communication quality with safety policy, reducing clarity.

NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Assigning DP-D to NGHE Solution would bypass architectural decomposition and leave packet loss mitigation unowned at the subsystem level, creating diffusion across multiple lower-level modules.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of DP-D because it encapsulates all teleoperation-specific communication quality mechanisms (FEC, retransmission, buffering, jitter compensation, QoS negotiation). The module has clear external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) that enable coordination with Vehicle, Safety Services, and network infrastructure without internal diffusion.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become a 'kitchen sink' if all communication quality concerns (not just teleoperation-specific) are assigned to it. Mitigation: Clearly scope Teleoperation to remote operation use cases; separate concerns like Vehicle-to-Cloud telemetry or inter-vehicle communication into distinct modules if needed.

Risk: Network Link interface may be underspecified, leading to ambiguity about who owns QoS negotiation (Teleoperation or Network Link). Mitigation: Define Network Link interface contract explicitly: Teleoperation requests QoS parameters, Network Link provides network condition feedback and confirms provisioning.

Risk: SafetyAlert interface may create tight coupling between Teleoperation and Safety Services if alerting logic becomes complex. Mitigation: Keep SafetyAlert interface simple and event-driven; Safety Services owns the policy for how to respond to packet loss alerts (e.g., operator notification, operational mode restriction).
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that the Network Link interface is owned by a distinct module (e.g., a Network Management or Infrastructure module) and that Teleoperation's role is to request and adapt to network conditions, not to provision or manage the network itself. This clarifies the boundary between communication quality (Teleoperation) and network infrastructure (Network Link owner).

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (multi-layered packet loss mitigation) directly addresses FR-NGHE-009 (packet loss ≤ customer-defined threshold). The DP provides multiple mechanisms (FEC, retransmission, QoS, buffering, monitoring, alerting) that collectively reduce packet loss and enable graceful degradation. The DP is implementable and testable against the acceptance criterion. No decoupling or missing mechanisms identified.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk (Low): Teleoperation module must coordinate with Network Link for QoS negotiation. This is a managed external dependency, not internal diffusion. Mitigation: Define Network Link interface contract clearly.

Diffusion Risk (Low): Teleoperation module must coordinate with Safety Services for operator alerting. This is appropriate given safety criticality. Mitigation: Keep SafetyAlert interface event-driven and simple.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 95% containment score for DP-D. All protocol-level mitigation logic (FEC, retransmission, buffering, jitter compensation) is cohesively owned by Teleoperation. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and necessary for system integration. No evidence of diffusion across multiple modules.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Packet loss can be measured directly via instrumentation of the teleoperation data path. Test scenarios can be designed to simulate customer-agreed network conditions. Acceptance criterion (packet loss ≤ customer-defined limit) is objective and measurable. Teleoperation module can be tested in isolation (with mock Vehicle and Safety Services) or integrated with full system.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: High. FR-NGHE-009 is allocated to Teleoperation module with clear ownership of packet loss mitigation. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-009.1 through 009.4) can be further allocated to specific sub-components within Teleoperation (e.g., protocol stack, monitoring service, alerting handler) or to external modules (e.g., Network Link for network provisioning). Allocation is traceable and verifiable.

recommended_revisions: Refine the Network Link interface contract to specify: (a) QoS parameters that Teleoperation can request, (b) network condition feedback that Network Link provides (latency, jitter, packet loss, available bandwidth), (c) confirmation of QoS provisioning or fallback behavior if QoS cannot be guaranteed.

Define the SafetyAlert interface contract to specify: (a) packet loss threshold events that trigger alerts, (b) alert severity levels (warning, critical), (c) expected response time for Safety Services to acknowledge and act on alerts.

Establish acceptance test plan with customer to define 'agreed network conditions' (e.g., 4G LTE with 50-100ms latency, 5% background packet loss, etc.) before detailed design. This removes ambiguity from the acceptance criterion.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-009 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-009.1 through 009.4) that address distinct concerns: (1) packet loss detection/reporting, (2) error correction/retransmission, (3) graceful degradation/alerting, (4) network provisioning. Each sub-FR requires further design and allocation. The selected DP-D is sound and the assigned IM (Teleoperation) is appropriate, but the requirement is complex enough to warrant decomposition to the next level. Proceeding with decomposition will clarify ownership of sub-concerns and reduce risk of missed design details.
decision.justification_detail: The high safety criticality of teleoperation packet loss and the multi-layered nature of DP-D (FEC, retransmission, QoS, buffering, monitoring, alerting) justify decomposition. Sub-FRs will enable more granular design, testing, and allocation of responsibilities within Teleoperation and across Safety Services and Network Link modules.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall detect and report packet loss events in real-time (ownership: Teleoperation module, monitoring sub-component).

FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall implement error correction or retransmission mechanisms to minimize effective packet loss impact on command execution (ownership: Teleoperation module, protocol stack sub-component).

FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall gracefully degrade or alert the operator when packet loss exceeds acceptable thresholds (ownership: Teleoperation module + Safety Services module, via SafetyAlert interface).

FR-NGHE-009.4: The network link shall be provisioned and monitored to maintain packet loss within customer-defined limits under agreed conditions (ownership: Network Link module or Infrastructure module, external to Teleoperation).

assumptions: Customer will define and agree on 'network conditions' (latency, jitter, bandwidth, background packet loss) before detailed design.
Teleoperation protocol stack is modular and can integrate adaptive FEC and retransmission without major redesign.
Network provider can support QoS negotiation and monitoring (or fallback to best-effort with local mitigation).
Operator alerting and graceful degradation do not introduce unacceptable latency that would worsen packet loss perception.
Safety Services module is available and can handle SafetyAlert events for operator notification and operational mode restriction.
Network Link module or equivalent infrastructure exists to provide QoS provisioning and network condition feedback.
open_risks: Risk: Vague 'agreed network conditions' could lead to ambiguous acceptance criteria and customer disputes. Mitigation: Establish acceptance test plan with customer early.
Risk: Legacy teleoperation protocol implementations may not support adaptive FEC or retransmission. Mitigation: Conduct protocol feasibility study during detailed design.
Risk: Public cellular networks (4G/5G) may not offer QoS guarantees. Mitigation: Design fallback strategies (e.g., local buffering, operator alerting) for best-effort networks.
Risk: Redundant communication paths (failover) increase cost and complexity. Mitigation: Evaluate cost-benefit against customer requirements and safety criticality.
Risk: Packet loss mitigation logic itself could introduce latency or jitter. Mitigation: Conduct latency analysis and performance testing during detailed design.
Risk: SafetyAlert interface may create tight coupling between Teleoperation and Safety Services. Mitigation: Keep interface simple and event-driven; define clear contract.
evidence_needed: Customer agreement on 'agreed network conditions' (latency, jitter, bandwidth, background packet loss profile).
Teleoperation protocol stack feasibility study: can FEC and retransmission be integrated? What is the latency impact?
Network infrastructure assessment: what QoS capabilities are available? What is the fallback strategy for best-effort networks?
Safety Services module design: how will packet loss alerts be handled? What operational restrictions will be imposed?
Acceptance test plan: detailed test scenarios, measurement methodology, acceptance criteria for packet loss under agreed network conditions.
Performance analysis: latency, jitter, and throughput impact of packet loss mitigation mechanisms.
Cost-benefit analysis: is redundant communication (failover) justified by safety criticality and customer requirements?
